mnemonics: The CLT is a 'Normal' Thing: Remember that the Central Limit Theorem (CLT) makes the sampling distribution of the mean normal, no matter the population's shape, as long as n is sufficiently large. Think: 'CLT Normalizes Things'.
keyPoints: The sampling distribution of a statistic (like the mean) is the distribution of that statistic, computed from all possible samples of the same size from a population.
The Central Limit Theorem (CLT) states that the sampling distribution of the sample mean will be approximately normal for large sample sizes (n), regardless of the population's distribution.
The standard deviation of the sampling distribution of the mean is called the standard error (SE) and is calculated as σ/√n, where σ is the population standard deviation.
commonMistakes: Confusing the standard deviation of the population (σ) with the standard error of the mean (σ/√n). The standard error is always smaller.
Assuming the sampling distribution is normal for a small sample size (n < 30) when the underlying population is not normally distributed. The CLT requires a 'large enough' n.
Thinking the sampling distribution describes the variation in a single sample. It describes the variation of a statistic (e.g., the mean) across many different samples.
